annotations.label: Mathematical proof

anchor.snippet: A mathematical proof is a deductive argument for a mathematical statement

annotations.note: Meta-mathematical concept describing what a proof is; not a mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Conjecture / hypothesis

anchor.snippet: A proposition that has not been proved but is believed to be true is known as a conjecture

annotations.note: Informal meta-mathematical notion with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal proof

anchor.snippet: A formal proof is a sequence of formulas in a formal language

annotations.note: The concept lives at the level of Lean's kernel; Mathlib has no named definition of 'formal proof' itself.

annotations.label: Direct proof

anchor.snippet: In direct proof, the conclusion is established by logically combining the axioms, definitions, and earlier theorems

annotations.note: Stylistic/meta description of a proof method, not a mathematical theorem.

annotations.label: Sum of two even integers is even

anchor.snippet: Consider two even integers x and y

annotations.note: Established as the additive `to_additive` version of `IsSquare.mul` in `Mathlib/Algebra/Group/Even.lean`, valid in any commutative semigroup.

annotations.label: Proof by mathematical induction

anchor.snippet: In proof by mathematical induction, a single "base case" is proved

annotations.note: Proof technique itself is not a Mathlib declaration; the underlying recursor is Lean's builtin `Nat.rec`.

annotations.label: Principle of mathematical induction

anchor.snippet: Let N = {1, 2, 3, 4, ... } be the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: The induction principle for ℕ is the core recursor `Nat.rec`, available throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: 2n − 1 is odd for all positive integers n

anchor.snippet: we can prove by induction that all positive integers of the form

annotations.note: The equivalent statement ¬ 2 ∣ 2*n − 1 (i.e. 2*n − 1 is odd) is proven for positive n in `Mathlib/Algebra/Group/Nat/Even.lean`.

annotations.label: Proof by contraposition

anchor.snippet: Proof by contraposition infers the statement

annotations.note: The contrapositive equivalence `¬a → ¬b ↔ b → a` (and its dot-notation form `Function.mtr`) underlies the technique.

annotations.label: If n² is even then n is even (contraposition)

anchor.snippet: contraposition can be used to establish that, given an integer

annotations.note: The iff `Even (n^k) ↔ Even n ∧ k ≠ 0` covers the n² case (k = 2).

annotations.label: Proof by contradiction

anchor.snippet: In proof by contradiction, also known by the Latin phrase reductio ad absurdum

annotations.note: A proof technique realized by the `by_contra` tactic and classical logic; not a Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Irrationality of √2

anchor.snippet: Suppose that

annotations.note: Stated and proved as `irrational_sqrt_two : Irrational (√2)`.

annotations.label: Proof by construction

anchor.snippet: Proof by construction, or proof by example, is the construction of a concrete example

annotations.note: Meta description of a proof style; not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Existence of transcendental numbers

anchor.snippet: Joseph Liouville , for instance, proved the existence of transcendental numbers

annotations.note: Liouville's construction of transcendental numbers is formalized via `Liouville.transcendental`.

annotations.label: Proof by exhaustion

anchor.snippet: In proof by exhaustion, the conclusion is established by dividing it into a finite number of cases

annotations.note: Proof-by-cases is a meta technique (e.g. `decide`, `Fin.cases`); no named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Four color theorem proof by exhaustion

anchor.snippet: the first proof of the four color theorem was a proof by exhaustion with 1,936 cases

annotations.note: No four-color-theorem statement was found in Mathlib (grep for `four_color` returns no hits).

annotations.label: Closed chain inference

anchor.snippet: A closed chain inference shows that a collection of statements are pairwise equivalent

annotations.note: Mathlib's `List.TFAE` ('the following are equivalent') captures the cyclic-equivalence pattern used in tactics like `tfae_cycle`.

annotations.label: Probabilistic proof

anchor.snippet: A probabilistic proof is one in which an example is shown to exist, with certainty, by using methods of probability theory

annotations.note: Meta description of a proof methodology, not a theorem statement.

annotations.label: Combinatorial proof

anchor.snippet: A combinatorial proof establishes the equivalence of different expressions by showing that they count the same object in different ways

annotations.note: A style of argument (double-counting/bijection), not a single formal statement.

annotations.label: Nonconstructive proof

anchor.snippet: A nonconstructive proof establishes that a mathematical object with a certain property exists

annotations.note: Meta concept; classical logic in Lean enables nonconstructive proofs but there is no named declaration.

annotations.label: Irrational a, b with a^b rational

anchor.snippet: The following famous example of a nonconstructive proof shows that there exist two irrational numbers

annotations.note: The √2^√2 argument is formalized as `not_irrational_rpow` in `Counterexamples/IrrationalPowerOfIrrational.lean`.

annotations.label: Undecidable statement

anchor.snippet: A statement that is neither provable nor disprovable from a set of axioms is called undecidable

annotations.note: Meta-logical notion; Mathlib's model-theory library can express provability but there is no general 'undecidable' definition.

annotations.label: Parallel postulate undecidable

anchor.snippet: One example is the parallel postulate

annotations.note: Mathlib has no axiomatic treatment of Euclidean geometry establishing independence of the parallel postulate.

annotations.label: Gödel's first incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel's (first) incompleteness theorem shows that many axiom systems of mathematical interest will have undecidable statements

annotations.note: Mathlib has model-theory infrastructure in `Mathlib/ModelTheory/Encoding.lean` but no proof of Gödel's first incompleteness theorem; `docs/1000.yaml` points to an external library.

annotations.label: Two-column proof

anchor.snippet: A particular way of organizing a proof using two parallel columns

annotations.note: Pedagogical proof presentation style with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.
